focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

By her count of the refrigerator cycles, it was close to ten o'clock and she was no closer.

Since seven-thirty she'd been sitting at Martin Kessler's kitchen table.

Her leather notebook lay open to a page that would've embarrassed her if any of her colleagues at the county office saw it. Across it she'd drawn a grid. The columns were labeled with initials: PD, CH, NG, DW, TR, JB. The rows were labeled with the physical evidence. Check marks, question marks, and, in two cases, small x's that meant confirmed absent crowded the page. The grid looked like a badly designed crossword puzzle, and it told her less than she wanted and more than she expected.

At last she took off her reading glasses and set them on the table. The beaded chain caught on her coffee mug and she untangled it with the patience of a woman who'd been untangling the same chain from the same kinds of things for a decade.

Martin Kessler was dead of cardiac arrest. The medical examiner had been clear: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, undiagnosed, aggravated by alcohol, sildenafil, and prolonged heat exposure from the hot tub. It was a natural death. People died like this. Healthy-looking men in their late forties dropped dead and their friends stood around looking stunned, and the medical explanation was usually sufficient.

But Martin Kessler's house had been rearranged while his body cooled.

With the glasses back on, she looked at her grid.

The body. Martin had been found in bed, and by the time the deputies arrived Saturday morning, he was on his back with his arms at his sides. Tidy. Composed. People who died in their sleep did not usually end up tidy and composed. They ended up in whatever position the last involuntary movement left them: twisted, half off a pillow, one arm thrown, a le...
open_hooks_to_carry: The group pushes Joel to explain the argument — the pressure has been building since Ch 4
Joel reveals: Martin was sleeping with his ex-wife Karen
The group's reaction treated like a fantasy football ethics debate — was it a violation? Is there a statute of limitations?
Paul's conflict: defending Martin while knowing Martin was wrong
Joel's catharsis — his anger finally has an audience and it is both genuine and absurd

payoffs_to_land.description: The argument from Ch 1 is explained — Martin was sleeping with Joel's ex-wife Karen. Joel's apparent suspiciousness dissolves as his motive (rage at a dead man) becomes sympathetically legible.

prev_tail_excerpt:
...n the chair arms. He looked not just upset but braced, like a man waiting for impact that had been delayed too long. Nate came out with his phone still in his hand and said something Cliff couldn't catch. Tommy flinched hard enough to slosh coffee over his knuckles.

"Jesus Christ," Tommy snapped.

"I just asked if you wanted more coffee," Nate said.

"Then ask from over there."

Nate threw up a hand and backed off. Tommy didn't apologize. He stood too fast, paced once to the far end of the deck, then came down the steps and cut around the side of the house. He stopped fifteen feet from Cliff without seeing him, bent at the waist, and spat into the grass. It wasn't a hangover spit either. It was a dry, sour heave that brought up almost nothing. Tommy stayed folded there, palms on his knees, staring at the ground until the spell passed.

Cliff held still.

When Tommy finally straightened, he looked toward the lake with the blank, ugly concentration of somebody trying not to come apart in public. Then he wiped his mouth with the back of his hand and walked back to the deck like nothing had happened.

That settled it better than any abstract thought Cliff had been dressing up as strategy. There would be no deck conversation tonight and no smooth opener over beers. If Cliff walked up to Tommy now with Martin's pages in his pocket and forty thousand dollars in his mouth, Tommy would hear only the first part. Maybe he caved. Maybe he lied. Maybe he did something dumber than either of those. Cliff had seen enough men at closing tables to know when the room had gone too hot for a clean ask.

His phone buzzed once. It wasn't Aaron, just the Gmail confirmation banner sliding down and disappearing. That was good enough for now.

He needed copies first, then the lawyer, then Tommy.
secondary_character_beats: Tommy should weigh in with coaching-mode moral certainty that rings hollow given what the reader now suspects
Doug should say almost nothing but his face should register something — he understands being betrayed by someone close
state_deltas_required: the group demanding Joel explain his argument with Martin
the group's loyalty to Martin splinters; Joel's motive becomes sympathetic; the group fractures along new lines
